@paulportesi Size matters for natural systems. Its competition for resources that end up limiting how much they grow. Human systems have been growing so much because we've been pruning competition for decades. The most special case being the State, which has no competition.

@dngeron @paulportesi And for that, the system must able to change over time, and that requires letting go of stuff that doesn't contribute to survival. 2/2

Impossível dizer quão estável seria, e por quanto duraria. É seguro assumir que ferramentas já utilizadas como o quantitative easing seriam expandidos.
Minha esperança é que tentativas de descentralizar o sistema financeiro (crypto) deem certo, e se ofereçam como alternativa. Quão provável isso é eu não sei.

Também importante: existe uma espécie de consenso (entre os big boys) hoje no mundo dos mercados/finanças de que a onda deflacionária da globalização/trabalho barato acabou e que a inflação global só vai aumentar, nos levando a anos de estagnação. Eu não acredito nisso. Por que?
Porque isso tudo ocorrerá em um contexto em que novas tecnologias super impactantes como I.A estão avançando cada vez mais rápido.

O que gerará outra onda deflacionária, que em última análise, é o que permitirá o uso de ferramentas que, em resumo, funcionam como espécie de aceleradores da economia. Ferramentas que são ou que na prática resultam em juros menores e mais liquidez.

Essa tendência, se extrapolada, nos coloca em um caminho de aceleração econômica global nunca antes vista na história, e de longe muito maior do que tudo já visto. Isso está de acordo com as tendências observadas na evolução da tecnologia da informação.

Não é à toa que sistemas que mais dependem de network effects são os mais afetados. (setores de tecnologia, é exatamente de vocês que eu falo)

A boa notícia é que tudo isso é rápido. 
A má notícia é que é rápido.

O maior desafio dos sistemas de governança do mundo hoje é adaptabilidade.

Esse problema é resultado de:

1) na escala do todo, a sociedade é um sistema informal, altamente adaptável (e nunca mudou tanto e tão rápido)
2) governos são sistemas formais, rígidos

O problema é, governos precisam ser rígidos, pois é necessário algum grau de segurança jurídica.

Então, como conciliar os dois? 

Escala!

governos menores são mais capazes de se alterarem a mudanças específicas na sociedade

Entretanto, no modelo atual Estado-Nação™ é que nele estão embutidos mecanismos que forçam a expansão do mesmo. Estados possuem mecanismos de incentivos internos que alinham o objetivo de seus agentes, de forma que todos que estão dentro se beneficiem com o aumento do Estado.

O resultado disso é uma bomba-relógio, em que Estados ao longo do tempo cada vez se tornarão mais e mais descolados da realidade social. E não seria a primeira vez que isso ocorre. Novos modelos de governança deverão surgir.

E não só isso, mas já existem sinais de um esgotamento do modelo. 
Apenas o fato de que políticas fiscais expansionistas já não darem o mesmo resultado de décadas atrás e a inversão da pirâmide demográfica já são o suficiente pra ligar o alerta.

Largue o pensamento de "tudo é como sempre foi e sempre vai continuar sendo". Essa é a maneira que seus avós precisavam pensar.

Nesse século, mudanças radicais acontecerão. E elas mal começaram.

In my opinion, the biggest problem humanity faces is emergent centralization due to economies of scale. 

Centralized arrangements benefit from economies of scale. 

How can we create a more interconnected society without the double-edged sword of economies of scale?

Notice how almost every dystopian scenario involves one or some centralized organization(s) having too much power. 

This is the problem we must solve.

My intuition tells me that the only way to solve the problem of emergent centralization is through technology that makes local production so cheap that the benefits of decentralized supply chains outweigh the costs. 

This would also improve society's ability to handle crises.

Another way to frame the problem is: "how can we create a society of equals without a state?"

However, I know two things for sure: 

Marxists got it all wrong and only builders - not intellectuals, politicians, or academics - will be able to solve this problem.

When we look at events of the past, it's easy to compress everything into a  'coherent' narrative.
However, when living in the present, all you have is just pieces of local information, and it's difficult to even discern their connections and impossible to predict their outcomes.

For instance, we're currently in the midst of an unprecedented cultural revolution, with the internet standing out as the most significant collective accomplishment of all time.

And, arguably, we are at the cusp of an even bigger revolution.

A singularity, if you will.

And yet, it doesn't feel like it.

We just get used to new things too fast. As if they always were there.

Some people would still say the the renaissance was the biggest cultural revolution ever, but compared to what is happening today, it's only a revolution for ants.

To try to take a peek into the future, you can't simply take the rate of change of the immediate past, and project into the future.

You must take into consideration that the rate of change is also changing. Accelerating.

Se você quer entender política de maneira objetiva, a coisa mais importante que precisa ser entendida é que o comportamento dos grupos não é aleatório, muito menos definidos pelo destino.  +

Embora não seja possível prever ou descrever de maneira 100% acurada o comportamento de indivíduos/grupos, é possível pelo menos saber a priori que os grupos que ocupam as posições de maior poder são aqueles que aplicam as estratégias viáveis de maneira mais eficiente. 

Metagame

Portanto, não é por coincidência que os maiores grupos apliquem as mesmas estratégias de maneira mais ou menos espelhada. Exemplificarei:

De um lado "pessoas de bem" vs "defensores dos pobres".

Duas maneiras distintas de sinalizar valores, mas que possuem a mesma função.

A função, entre outras coisas menores, é ser um filtro e meio de coesão.

"Se você não se considera X, então não é um de nós!"

"Se você não concorda com nós, então não é X!"

Funciona.

Se não funcionasse não seria o padrão observado. E esse padrão sobreviveu ao tempo.

Essas ferramentas intragrupo têm absolutamente ZERO relação com as consequências reais das ações desses grupos quando no poder.

"Pessoas de bem" defendem a guerra às drogas, que gera mais violência.

"Defensores dos pobres" defendem planejamento central, que gera mais pobreza.

Agentes que possuem poder real (não necessariamente políticos) entendem que o que interessa de verdade é como ideias afetam o comportamento real das pessoas. Se essas ideias são verdadeiras ou pelo menos verificáveis, não importa.

A boa notícia é que isso simplifica tudo: pra entender o objetivo de um agente, basta se perguntar que comportamento ele quer instigar nos interlocutores.

Também assuma que todos eles são psicopatas, simplesmente por ser uma característica muito útil nesse ramo.
É isso, muito obrigado por comparecerem à minha palestra. Espero que seja de alguma valia.

Sistemas complexos só podem sobreviver e serem de fato complexos na medida em que existe algum tipo de barreira, ou separação ('boundary') entre as partes que o compõe. 

Nossos órgãos são feitos de células. Nossos corpos são feitos de órgãos. Sociedades são feitas de indivíduos+

Em sociedade, existem 2 dimensões da identidade do indivíduo. A interna, e externa. 

A interna é como o indivíduo se vê, e o que ele quer se tornar. A externa é como seus atos interagem com os atos e percepção alheios. É a interação entre as 'boundaries'.

Existe uma maneira certeira de destruir uma civilização: negar a existência de uma das dimensões.

A negação da interna gera uma sociedade incapaz de mudança, com indivíduos uniformes demais e muito avessos a risco. Em outras palavras, a 'boundary' é fina demais.

Já a negação da externa gera uma sociedade sem apego a padrões de comportamento. É uma sociedade que não respeita seus processos evolucionários, e  pessoas se tornam incapazes de regular o comportamento uma das outras.

Em outras palavras, a boundary é espessa demais.

A theory of mine is that the rise of anxiety/depression in the last decades is mostly due increasingly living in sterile environments, and the anxiety/depression is only a reaction of the body's risk management systems 🧵

When talking about risk, always take in consideration reward.

This is a rough model (too simplistic, of course) of how the body creates response to risk using emotions https://t.co/lVCMZ2jRTG

Sterile environments like school, academia or offices cut reward immensely due to their predictive nature. Risk is also artificial, as it is also completely predictable.

Your tasks, tests, etc have schedule and you always know what you are going to face.

How do you expect your body to react in environments like this? 

Chronic exposure to such environments make your body adapted to it, thus, you will experience more commonly anxiety and maybe depression

Depression is, I believe, "energy-saving mode".

Think of rainy days. People get sad, low motivation, sleepy, etc

The outer environment just got riskier. What if you slip and fall and break an arm? animals tend to hide, so less possible reward

it makes sense to just conserve energy, now imagine EVERY day is rainy

The idea here is simply that, in an environment like school, academia or an office, real risk and reward is removed (or at least flattened), thus making your body, over time, adapt to it by always being an an energy-saving mode.

The idea that love is "just a bunch of chemical reactions" is not just myopic, it's wrong.

Love is a system-wide, emergent phenomenon. It happens at the interplay between various systems. Myofascial, limbic, nervous, sexual systems, even your digestive system plays a role.

The chemical reactions happen BECAUSE of love, through the means of downward causation, not the other way around! It can't be reduced.

Now tell me: how does standard science can study something like this if trying to isolate causes is precisely the wrong way to investigate it?

Furthermore, why love?

From all the ways matter can behave, from all ways we can feel, from all ways we could connect to each other, why love?

What does it have, or doesn't have, that is so special?

There is no law of physics saying that death is unavoidable. I think it exists for 2 reasons: 

1. Self repair systems are energetically costly
2. In the paradigm of evolution by natural selection, the existence of death is necessary for evolution itself

However:

1. We are getting better and better at acquiring energy, and will probably get way better
2. Evolution now happens through technological and cultural progress, through its own mechanisms of selection

Eventually, it will only be a matter of engineering to design bodies that can keep repairing themselves as long as there is enough energy, making one's lifespan indefinite

just realized something very important and simple about my thinking style that so far I have not been able to express

I tend a lot to think in terms of "this is what I'm likely to think, doesn't mean I'm really *thinking* it"

I know its confusing, but if you ask my opinion of something, it won't be really "my" opinion as I don't identify myself with it

my answer will be to the question "what is likely to be your opinion given what I'm asking and your world models?"

you can say: "wait, but everybody is like this, duh!", but the thing is that I have been aware of this for a long time but for it was difficult to put into words, and probably still is, as I'm certain people reading this will find it confusing

the awareness part is what matters

there's this part of psychological development where you start to realize that social roles are kinda fake

I suspect most people dont break through it and get stuck in either believing that then nothing matters or maybe worse: "they can be whatever I/"we" want!"

A way to advance is to develop a system of principles, so you align the roles you play with those higher ideas. Those people tend to be  genuine and really care about "the truth"

But their reasoning breaks when there's contradictions between their principles and reality

and they can get stuck by choosing to strongly identify with those principles/"ideology".

To advance, you need to create several systems of principles. So you can look at them as simply tools that help you navigate the world, and switch between them when necessary.

the thing is, this might look unprincipled but this is fruit of the realization that you cant actually have a set of principles that is comprehensive enough because the world is too complex

multiprincipled, if you will

this road leads to the understanding  that all of this doesn't apply only to social roles, but to even to what you consider the most objective aspects of reality

"even water's wetness isn't absolute"

being fake doesn't mean its less real or less meaningful

When I was 6 or 7 years old, I had a friend with whom I played with after school. One day, we had a discussion: I thought the moon followed me, and he thought that the moon followed him. To test it, we decided that we would walk in opposite directions, both looking at the moon +

and then we did it. We both walked back to our original positions saying the same thing: 'See? The moon followed ME!'.

I was surprised. Not by the weird contradiction that the moon was apparently following both, but by the fact that I was the only one aware that it was weird

He was adamant, saying that I was lying, and I was confused.

I can't remember exactly what I thought, but the feeling remained: "there is something bigger going on, why can't he see it?"

I'm here telling the story simply because I think this feeling is very important to the evolution of what I usually call my 'self'. How exactly I don't know, but its something that I feel deeply, almost everyday.

I wish I could be surrounded by people who get it. By people who  have the humility to understand when the problem is not knowledge, but perspective. And not merely "what" perspective, but how big it is. People who know that you need to look up, not merely to the sides.

Could maybe Kegan levels be reframed as sophistication of the ability to imitate?

S2 to S3 -> goes from imitating basically everything to selective imitation, which allows for learning specific patterns of behaviors tied to group identity (when to NOT imitate)

S3 to S4 -> learning control of selective imitation itself (individuation?), which allows for identification of what is pure imitation and what is not, which allows for building of an individual system of values not merely based on imitation

S4 to S5 -> By controlling individuation itself, one becomes a master of many internal perspectives, fluidly subordinating these models of the world to be used as tools for interacting with the world. The center of identity thus becomes the >ability< to create an internal world.

the sense of wonder that you feel when learning more general ways to interpret the world is actually the feeling of 𝐢𝐧𝐭𝐞𝐫𝐜𝐨𝐧𝐞𝐜𝐭𝐞𝐝𝐧𝐞𝐬𝐬, which is also the feeling of 𝐫𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐫𝐞𝐧𝐜𝐞

pursuing these feelings will lead you to cultivate a deep sense of humility

when you learn something through reading, you are doing it through language, or

in other words, through a representation in a somewhat shared representational system

you are learning a language within a language

given the differences in how models are represented, its absolutely no wonder that the former likes complexity, and the latter is reductionist

the idea here is simply that language compresses models by removing dimensionality

In network dynamics, reaching the percolation threshold induces criticality, which may then trigger a phase transition. If the phase transition results in a qualitatively higher level of organization then the system has undergone a metasystem transition.

Percolation Threshold → A network reaches a critical connectivity level where a giant component forms, allowing large-scale interaction.

Criticality → The system enters a state where small perturbations can trigger large cascading effects.
Phase Transition → If the system undergoes a fundamental change in its macroscopic behavior (sudden shift in functionality, structure, or emergent behavior), a phase transition occurs.
Metasystem Transition – If the phase transition results in a qualitatively higher level of colletive intelligence, then the system has undergone a metasystem transition.

A metasystem transition is not merely a shift in behavior within the same systemic level. It implies an evolutionary leap to a higher level of control, where the system itself becomes a subsystem of a larger, more complex structure.

schelling point life-cycle: cursed edition

the vibe — ambient alignment. people feel it, can't name it. shared orientation without articulation.

the echo — small clusters notice each other. indirect signaling. the thing becomes almost speakable.

the crossfire — others catch on. interpretations diverge. it gets pulled into conflict, parody, backlash.

the ruin — overexposed, overused, misused. loses coherence. either collapses into noise or becomes an empty shell.
the residue — traces linger. symbolic gravity remains, but the original coordination function is gone. haunted by its own memetic history.

@nosilverv as a network grows, it stops being a flat mesh of loosely connected nodes and starts forming clusters. these clusters emerge around high-connectivity nodes—SCHELLING POINTS/hubs—that act as local centers of gravity.

@nosilverv early on, connections are sparse and evenly distributed. information diffuses slowly, and influence is decentralized. but as more nodes join, preferential attachment kicks in: new nodes tend to link to already well-connected ones. the rich get richer, structurally.

@nosilverv tl;dr: growth produces structure. structure creates centers. centers attract attention, and attention reshapes the network.

imagine a flock of birds. it's movements are patterned but somewhat random, right? they aren't really purposeful, but it is definetely emergent from local interations between the birds. its less like a living being, more like a whirlpool, right?

1/ https://t.co/dQB6gawwdv

now imagine that we add a central bird: one that gives commands that can rewrite the *rules* of interbird interaction. wouldn't this allow for the emergent patterns to be more purposeful? wouldn't you tell that now the flock is more alive than before?

before, the flock was reactive: its form an echo of wind, predators, internal spacing, but now, it's proactive. it can anticipate. the central bird can set the terms by which movements coalesce for specific *goals*

and if the central bird can learn, then the system enters a new regime. it starts to resemble a mind, or at least a proto-mind.

so yes, the flock becomes more alive. not because its more complex, but because it gains the ability to BEND EMERGENCE TOWARDS GOALS.

1. the holy grail of governance is creating a system resilient to ideological takeover. the problem isn’t our ideas, it’s that current systems reward specific ideas, like how democracy favors vote-buying populism.

2. we need institutions that get instant feedback from society—not just public opinion, but real-world consequences from policies. market forces seem promising, but private governance is vulnerable to coercive force.
3. the challenge: governance that adapts without consolidating power, while still being robust enough to resist external takeovers. private models are decentralized but often lack defense mechanisms.

4. resilient governance must balance adaptability and stability, combining decentralized decision-making with collective defense. cybernetically, it needs modularity, accurate feedback, robust info flows, and resistance to coercion

I think the Free Energy Principle framing is slightly off. Let me reframe it:

1/ Every system with a boundary must produce just enough entropy to maintain itself - not too little (collapses) not too much (dissolves). Like climbing an infinite escalator.
2/ As systems become more complex internally, their minimum viability threshold increases. A soap bubble needs minimal energy. A cell needs active metabolism. An organism needs a nervous system.
3/ More internal complexity means more fragility - more components to coordinate, more ways things can go wrong, tighter tolerances required to maintain organization.

4/ Cognition emerges as the solution to fragility. Complex systems need sensors to detect problems, prediction to avoid them, coordination to balance competing needs, and fine control to stay within viability limits.
5/ This reframes the Free Energy Principle. It's not just about minimizing surprise - it's that complexity itself drives up the baseline maintenance requirements.

6/ This is why (I think), in this universe, cognition will always emerge. If there are conditions for higher complexities (life), then cognition emerges because it is the universal solution for sophisticated boundary maintenance.
7/ The escalator doesn't just go down - it speeds up as you get more organized. Cognition is what allows complex systems to run fast enough to stay in place.

"Spiritual practices that work" create predictable state changes that unlock different modes of information processing. But they're not just nervous system hacks - they're climbing a hierarchy toward states with greater ontological authority.
Body vs mind spiritual paths are different approaches to the same destination: accessing higher-order states of consciousness that have clearer views of reality than ordinary awareness.

The ability to shift between states proves the "self" isn't solid - but more importantly, it reveals there's a hierarchy. Some states have ontological privilege over others, like standing on a mountain vs being stuck in a valley.

Your sense of self is subordinated to whatever state you're currently in. But there's a meta-state - pure awareness recognizing itself - that has authority over all other configurations without being touched by them.

Spiritual practice is about recognizing and stabilizing access to that witness consciousness from which all other states are visible. It's climbing the hierarchy toward whatever has the clearest view of the whole system.
The real mastery isn't staying in blissed-out states, it's maintaining access to that ontological high ground while fully engaging with whatever's arising. Being the sky that contains all weather.

insights about fairness:

organisms organized in groups evolve fairness sensors to detect threats to collective cohesion. when capuchins reject unequal pay, they're not making moral claims - they're responding to perturbations that historically predicted group dissolution

unfairness = signal that the meta-organism's boundary conditions are degrading. like pain but for social bodies. probably phylogenetically older than explicit fairness "rules" which are just post-hoc regularization
this explains why:

fairness varies wildly across cultures (different group structures = different vulnerabilities)
perfect equality feels creepy and unstable (systems need controlled unfairness for adaptive capacity, cf. potlatch/carnivale)

fairness discourse is exhausting (using consciousness for what emergence should handle)
revolutionary fairness programs consistently fail (they're fighting homeostasis)

the Individual Autonomy stuff libertarians love (me too actually) might just be group-autonomy dynamics at n=1. we retrofitted philosophical justifications onto what were always just persistence mechanisms at various scales

tldr: fairness isn't about justice, it's about maintaining the operational closure of social systems. which sounds cybernetic bc it is - living groups are information-processing entities trying to minimize surprise re: their own dissolution

the physical vs mental copying distinction is correct but incomplete.

physical copying (children, training, institutions) has high roi constraints: long timelines, high resource demands, defection risk.
→ so mental copying via repeated exposure to shared texts becomes dominant
but mental copying is lossy. it creates functional similarity only within specific domains. outside those, behavior drifts. so full coordination via mental copying alone doesn’t scale.

the missing piece: coordination doesn’t just scale through direct copying. it scales via nested similarity hierarchies.

dissimilar groups can coordinate internally through similarity, and externally via structural relationships.

think: tissue types → organs → bodies. same with individuals → families → clans → tribes → nations.
each level uses different similarity bases because information degrades with social distance. shared values at one level, shared symbols or laws at another.

key constraint: coordination scales inversely with behavioral flexibility.
cells coordinate easily because they’re behaviorally fixed (chemically hardwired). humans are flexible, so we need artificial constraint mechanisms (e.g. religions, laws, rituals).

but most human coordination isn’t similarity-based. it’s specialization-based. markets, bureaucracies, families run on role complementarity, just not cloned behavior.
so the real selection pressure isn’t just “make copies.” it’s: "build coordination mechanisms."

sometimes those mechanisms are similarity-based (armies, cults). sometimes they’re specialization-based (labor divisions). sometimes hierarchical (corporations, states)
copying pressure exists because full behavioral constraint is computationally intractable at scale
result: nested coordination hierarchies, where sacred texts = shared decision trees = predictable behavior within bounded domains.

this is meta-commentary. what bothers me isn’t the post itself (it's fine). it’s how attraction discourse collapses when people don’t realize there are different developmental stages involved, each with its own language, logic, and desire https://t.co/S59QHQjw7e

the way people talk about “what women want” (or “what men want”) often assumes a single frame of reference. but people are not equally developed. structurally or "psychospiritually"

so they’re not even playing the same game.

some people are still seeking externally: security, validation, survival, status. others have started orienting from within: coherence, alignment, something like dharma.

neither is wrong. but they’re not equivalents.

so when someone writes from a stage where inner coherence feels central, and frames it as “this is what women want,” it gets messy.

because some women do. others don’t. some can’t even perceive it yet.

what gets lost in these debates is that attraction isn’t a constant. it changes as your center of gravity shifts. you start resonating with different things, seeing beauty in different signals.

development reconfigures desire.
it’s recursive. at each layer of selfhood, you seek someone who fits into the world as you perceive it. and as your world changes, so does your pattern of attraction.

we don’t talk about this enough. not in honest ways. we argue about surface mismatches while the real split is structural.

no one’s wrong. they’re just in different stages of the spiral, using the same words with different meanings.

i hope there’s space for better public thinkers who understand this. who can speak clearly across layers without bitterness or performance. not to go viral, but to make sense.

most of what we have now is noise.

the bitterness in discourse bothers me. it’s like everyone’s speaking past each other, but instead of slowing down, they just double down. louder. meaner. more certain.

as if confusion were a moral failing.

this isn’t really about women. it’s about development, recursion, misattunement, and the painful experience of not being seen from the same level you're speaking from.

it just looks like it’s about dating.

the newtonian metaphor sees trauma as a force: something acts on you, creates tension, leaves a wound. very action-reaction. very linear. trauma “pulls you back,” “weighs you down,” “holds you in place.”

but if we go relativistic...

it doesn’t just “pull” you. it alters the shape of your possible movements. it curves the paths your thoughts and behaviors can take. like how light curves around a black hole, your attention and perception bend around the trauma.

you’re still moving forward, but through a warped coordinate system.
straight lines (what “normal” behavior looks like from the outside) aren’t straight anymore in your frame. others might say “why don’t you just X?” but from inside, X it’s uphill, sideways, maybe unreachable.

further:

the closer you are to the trauma, the slower time feels. it dilates. moments repeat. loops.

from the outside, others perceive you as frozen, or irrational. from the inside, you're stuck in geodesics that feel inevitable.

capitalism isn’t default. but it also isn’t arbitrary. it’s what happens when primates with language, surplus energy, and time start solving coordination problems at scale. you get property. you get prices. you get systems that offload decision-making into signals because the alternative is stagnation or failure.

joint stock companies and IP law are recent hacks. but they sit on top of ancient constraints. how do you align strangers. how do you allocate limited resources when no one has the full picture. how do you make feedback travel faster than error propagation.

you can change the surface. you can write new laws. but the pressure that shaped the old ones is still here. ignore that and the system either reverts or breaks in worse ways.

social systems evolve. you don’t get to skip the constraints. only reroute them.

@halogen1048576 yea, but that fuzziness isn't unique to capitalism. it's true of almost any large-scale adaptive system. "democracy," "religion," even "science" have no clean definitions that hold across time and culture. they’re clusters of evolving practices, constraints, and affordances that…

yea, but that fuzziness isn't unique to capitalism. it's true of almost any large-scale adaptive system. "democracy," "religion," even "science" have no clean definitions that hold across time and culture. they’re clusters of evolving practices, constraints, and affordances that stabilize under certain conditions.

what makes capitalism hard to define is that it isn’t a single coherent system. it’s a family of responses to coordination problems that arise once you hit a certain threshold of complexity. scarcity, distributed knowledge, incentive misalignment, delayed feedback, etc

some of those responses got formalized into law, like joint stock companies. others emerged as norms, like wage labor or credit. the legal wrappers change, but the functional pressures are consistent. so arguing over the “true” definition kind of misses the point. "capitalism" is an attractor.

@fluxlesss the swarm isn't anarchic. every agent in torus operates within delegated permission scopes that create hard boundaries on what they can do.

when an agent delegates permissions, the recipient can only act within those constraints. this creates recursive alignment: each delegation…

the swarm isn't anarchic. every agent in torus operates within delegated permission scopes that create hard boundaries on what they can do.

when an agent delegates permissions, the recipient can only act within those constraints. this creates recursive alignment: each delegation layer inherits the constraints from above, plus any new ones. so agents naturally work toward higher-level goals without needing to understand them.

the coherence mechanism is economic. agents that produce value receive more emission delegations. agents that fail lose them. this selection pressure operates at every edge of the delegation graph simultaneously. bad actors get pruned, effective patterns get reinforced.

what makes it work is delegation creating consequence-sharing. success and failure propagate through delegation chains. agents delegate portions of their emission streams to specialists who amplify the swarm’s overall performance. better performance attracts more emissions from above, aligning incentives at every level.

so coherence comes from recursive constraint propagation (through permissions), plus economic selection through delegation chains. not from hoping llms spontaneously coordinate, but from structuring how authority and resources flow through the system.

the shortest definition of life i can give right now is simply "recursive self-steerable emergence"

think from a designers perspective: if you want to create a complex system that works at a specific scale, there is a fact that is both a blessing and a curse -- local…

the shortest definition of life i can give right now is simply "recursive self-steerable emergence"

think from a designers perspective: if you want to create a complex system that works at a specific scale, there is a fact that is both a blessing and a curse -- local interactions generate global behavior

why curse? because in the least the system is constrained by the lower scales of its organization, so in a sense IT WILL DO AS IT WANTS.

blessing? because this gives you a way to work around scaling issues related to global control

life discovered how to make emergence steerable through hierarchical control emerging from the substrate. local interactions become parametrically steerable by higher-order structure that can modulate the interaction rules themselves. (read that again)

instead of fixed interaction rules that generate one type of collective behavior, you get hub mechanisms that can dynamically adjust those parameters based on global state. local agents still follow simple protocols, but now some subset can modify the constraints that generate the collective dynamics. this breaks you out of static optimization landscapes. the system can explore different behavioral regimes by changing its own rules in real-time.

and hubs can be stacked. the behavior of a network of hubs can itself be steerable by higher-order hubs. you get hierarchies of control where each level modulates the parameters of the level below it, creating nested recursive loops of self-steering emergence

swarms are fault-tolerant because they're distributed, but programmable swarms are evolutionarily stable because they can adapt their collective computation. the hub doesn't micromanage, instead it broadcasts low-dimensional control signals that exploit the natural modes of the local dynamics. this is why swarm formation keeps emerging across evolution: it's not just distributed optimization. it's *reconfigurable* distributed optimization.
of course you get self-similarity across scales. as above so below. how would it not echo? when emergence becomes recursive, creation starts resembling its own hand

there’s a hidden clause in the 2nd law: if disorder increases overall, then any local order must pay for its existence by channeling dissipation *through* itself.
 
this means that boundary evolution must come before self-replication mechanisms evo, so through a first principles…

there’s a hidden clause in the 2nd law: if disorder increases overall, then any local order must pay for its existence by channeling dissipation *through* itself.
 
this means that boundary evolution must come before self-replication mechanisms evo, so through a first principles approach:
 
1. everything falls apart over time.
2. things that last need a boundary to slow that decay.
3. some molecules (like fats) naturally form bubbles in water.
4. these bubbles grow by grabbing more fats from around them.
5. mechanical instability splits them at critical size
6. each new bubble keeps some of the old stuff inside.
7. if what’s inside helps the bubble grow faster (think autocatalytic loops here), that combo spreads.
8. inside bubbles, stuff stays concentrated, so rare chemical tricks happen more often.
9. one of those tricks was making molecules that could copy themselves (like RNA).
10. from there you get the normal natural selection we know about
 
this flips the standard story. instead of "replication emerged then got compartmentalized," it's "compartments replicated then got genetic control."

the boundary IS the first replicator!

there’s a hidden clause in the 2nd law: if disorder increases overall, then any local order must pay for its existence by channeling dissipation *through* itself.
 
this means that boundary evolution must come before self-replication mechanisms evo, so through a first principles…

although i align myself with the sentiment (more kids good), @elonmusk seems to misunderstand fundamental stuff here. 

it sounds simple: tweak the drives -> get the outcome. but that’s not how collective behavior works. at all.

the desire to reproduce is already there. biology handled that long ago. what determines birth rates isn’t raw drive, it’s the structure wrapped around it: housing costs, career tradeoffs, cultural expectations, state policy, etc (no one even knows exactly and the weight of each is dynamic). the drive is the spark, the means through which it can happen, but the network of incentives decides whether the spark catches. think of it as the network itself constantly tweaking the weight of the drive.

think about it: in rich countries, people still want sex, but kids are expensive, time-consuming, and socially risky. the limbic system didn’t vanish, the network made children harder to fit.

so fertility is not a knob to be twisted at the base of the system. it’s an emergent property of the *whole* web. and webs like this DON'T YIELD to central commands. every attempt to “program” them runs into the same problem central planners face: the system adapts around the knob, because control only works when it’s embedded in the network itself.

AI will be another node in that network, but not a god sitting outside it. if you try to engineer metasystemic outcomes directly (“we’ll program birth rates”), you miss the lesson of every adaptive system: real steering comes from recursive alignment inside the network, where each layer of incentives stays tied to the ones above and below. never from hacking the monkey brain.

i think actually no job will be safe. high or low income. although first i think the high income jobs will be the first to be replaced. but that doesn't necessarily mean UBI.

what happens is that the layers split. execution gets automated, and humans shift to a layer where the main role is resource allocation.

the important question is not who has a job, but who steers flows of energy and meaning. once machines can handle execution, the open problem is deciding what gets funded, what gets prioritized, how incentives tie together. that becomes the layer humans occupy.

and this is not really different from how things already work. today we think of income as coming from the job itself, but in practice it comes from how we allocate our own labor. most of the time we are the ones directing resources toward ourselves by choosing and performing a task, so it feels like the job is the source of value. once execution is automated, that illusion falls away and the allocation layer becomes more visible.

ai will expand the range of what can be executed, but it will not remove humans from the loop. it just changes where agency sits. labor can be automated, valuation cannot.

i get what you mean. but i think you’re collapsing local rules with pure locality.

cells follow local rules, sure, but they’re bathed in global signals: hormones, nervous impulses, vascular flows. there are routing hubs everywhere: lymph nodes, ganglia, endocrine glands. the whole-body coherence comes from layers of nested control centers coordinating across scales, not from each cell just vibing with its neighbors.

same with starlings: works for a few birds because physics itself does the routing (sightlines, reaction times). try scaling that to millions with multiple goal conflicts. you start needing air-traffic-control-like hubs or the flock fragments.

so yeah, local interaction rules are necessary, but they’re not sufficient for metasystemic intelligence. without emergent centers to compress, route, and delegate, you simply don't get the collective behavior you are expecting

1) on resources
yes, i get the knowledge point. but being better at using resources in the future doesn’t erase the constraints in the present. coordination systems have to work under current scarcity conditions or they’re just wishful thinking.

the current relative level of scarcity is still a problem regardless of the existence of a future where we summon resources with our minds. the cool part is that the total amount of money can change based on changes of relative levels of scarcity.

2) on artificial scarcity + local interactions
yes, i see the distinction you’re making. but artificial scarcity isn’t automatically bad. sometimes you need it to allocate finite bandwidth, attention, compute, etc. the real issue is that you’re only looking at local interactions. complexity forces longer-range routing, and that’s where coordination problems explode.

3) on the “extraction” framing
yes, i get the game-theory angle. but adversarial dynamics aren’t tied only to monetary scarcity. power, information, and control layers all generate similar dynamics even if you had infinite tokens. money is just one substrate where this shows up.

and EVERY ecosystem will have parasites. complain with god, not with me

4) on boundary resilience
if a system can’t even survive contact with its environment, then reality itself is refuting it. design elegance is irrelevant if it fails the survival test.

5)on scaling

I think what you're calling an "egregore" is actually swarm dynamics

The emergent mind and goal-directedness of these groups comes from individual goals composing into higher-order goals

When agents with their own goals interact, their goals compose through the specific ways those agents are connected. A company looks like it has unified intent because individual goals (get paid, get promoted, avoid getting fired) compose through formal structures into something that acts coherent. A society's goals emerge from millions of people pursuing their own interests through local interactions

The network structure between agents determines how their goals can compose. Dense, hierarchical connections create different composite goals than sparse, distributed ones

So yes, companies, teams, societies, and individuals all show goal-directedness. But they're not all the same kind of mind. They're different swarms where individual agency composes differently based on coupling topology

I wonder how much predictive power would come from formalizing how goals compose through different network structures. This would be very interesting for Open Memetics.
